ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for Singaporean red bean kueh; closest match is the generic bean cake food product class. Verified against FoodOn ontology browser.
ontology.foodex2_description: Sweet bean paste confection — steamed cake with red bean filling
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Singaporean red bean kueh. A0CKP (sweet confectionery, cereal-based) is the closest defensible anchor. Exact match unavailable in FoodEx2 v2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for red bean cake or kueh category as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of red bean cake is approximately 80 g (one piece), as commonly portioned in Singapore hawker stalls and traditional kueh shops.

nutrient_source.title: Red Bean Cake (Kueh) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values are accepted as-is from the HPB Foodo database entry for Red Bean Cake (F11090051). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for adzuki bean cake (NDB 19304) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for sweetened red bean confection
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean red bean kueh in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 67 is adopted from the closest analogous entry: sweetened red bean paste confection (adzuki bean cake, GI ~67, medium), consistent with published values for similar East/Southeast Asian bean-paste cakes in Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (67 × 45.8) / 100 ≈ 30.7, where available carbohydrate per 80 g serving = 57.3 × 0.80 = 45.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (40.6 g/100 g) with moderate sodium (72 mg/100 g); excess dietary sugar independently elevates blood pressure via insulin resistance and sympathetic nervous system activation (AHA 2018).
hypertension.guidance: Despite low sodium, the extremely high sugar content (40.6 g/100 g) poses an independent cardiovascular risk for hypertensive individuals; limit to very small occasional portions and prioritise unsweetened red bean preparations.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 40.6 g/100 g far exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling; medium GI of 67 combined with high carbohydrate load (57.3 g/100 g) and moderate fibre (4.97 g/100 g) produces severe post-meal glucose excursions.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Red bean cake is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high sugar content (40.6 g/100 g) and medium-high glycaemic load; avoid entirely and substitute with unsweetened whole red beans which provide fibre and protein without the glycaemic burden.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat of approximately 5.5 g/100 g from shortening or butter, combined with very high sugar (40.6 g/100 g), contributes to atherogenic dyslipidaemia and endothelial inflammation relevant to CVD risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high saturated fat (~5.5 g/100 g) and very high sugar (40.6 g/100 g) makes red bean cake a food to limit for individuals managing cardiovascular disease; consume only occasionally in small portions as part of a diet otherwise low in saturated fat and added sugar.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar at 40.6 g/100 g promotes pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6) relevant to RA flares; estimated saturated fat (~5.5 g/100 g) further amplifies inflammatory signalling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Red bean cake should be avoided during rheumatoid arthritis flares due to its very high sugar and saturated fat content, both of which promote systemic inflammation; choose unsweetened red bean preparations as a lower-risk alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Red bean cake (known in Malay as Kuih Kacang Merah and in Chinese as 红豆糕 / 紅豆糕) is a traditional Singaporean and Malay sweet confection made primarily from cooked adzuki (red) beans, sugar, glutinous rice flour or wheat flour, and shortening or butter. The cake is typically steamed or baked into a dense, moist block or individual portions with a soft, slightly chewy texture and a sweet, earthy red bean flavour. It is a popular snack in Singapore hawker centres, traditional kueh shops, and festive occasions. Nutritionally, red bean cake is energy-dense at 411 kcal per 100 g, with a high carbohydrate content of 57.3 g/100 g, of which 40.6 g is sugar — one of the highest sugar concentrations in the local snack category. Protein is moderate at 10.6 g/100 g, contributed by the red beans, and dietary fibre is 4.97 g/100 g. Fat is 15.6 g/100 g with an estimated saturated fat of approximately 5.5 g/100 g from the shortening or butter component. Sodium is relatively low at 72 mg/100 g. The glycaemic index is approximately 67 (medium), yielding a high glycaemic load per serving (~30.7 per 80 g piece). Clinically, red bean cake is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood glucose due to its very high sugar content and medium-high glycaemic load. The high sugar and saturated fat content also make it a food to limit for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. It is a significant pro-inflammatory food for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. Unsweetened whole red beans are a nutritionally superior alternative, providing the same fibre and protein benefits without the glycaemic burden.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics eat red bean cake?
How many calories in red bean kueh?
Is red bean cake suitable for hypertension?
Red bean cake GI glycemic index

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F11090051) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous entry (adzuki bean cake) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item. GI adopted from the closest analogous entry in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 for sweetened red bean confection. All mandatory multilingual names and aliases are present. Ontology anchors are defensible approximations with documented gap notes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (5.5 g/100 g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entry for adzuki bean cake (NDB 19304 / SR Legacy), as the HPB Foodo database does not separately report saturated fat for this item. This value should be replaced with a direct HPB measurement if one becomes available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this traditional Singaporean red bean kueh preparation are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database (F11090051) or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI of 67 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 for sweetened red bean confection; no direct GI measurement for Singaporean red bean kueh was found in the approved GI sources. 4. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for Singaporean red bean kueh as of 2026. 5. Dietary flag is_vegan set to false as butter or dairy-based shortening is commonly used; is_halal set to true assuming no lard or alcohol-based ingredients, consistent with common Malay kueh preparation.
